interactions.summary: Jiang interprets the Priam death scene as violent, almost pornographic Roman poetry that negates the Iliad's lesson of forgiving the enemy.
The student reads Priam's death and Aeneas' reaction: the king becomes a nameless corpse, Aeneas freezes, thinks of his family, sees Helen, and burns with revenge.
Jiang extracts the new anti-Odyssey lesson from Aeneas' reaction: where the Odyssey treats love as redemptive return, the Aeneid makes love the cause of civilizational destruction and corruption.
He begins the Roman family hierarchy: Venus redirects Aeneas from revenge to saving family, but the order is father first, inheriting son second, wife as follower.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 reads quoted poem; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang explaining. Prompts to keep reading are lecture management, not public questions.

claims.claim: Jiang calls the Aeneid's violence almost pornographic and says Romans loved it because, in his characterization, they are bloodthirsty.
Priam's death negates the Iliad's moral lesson of forgiving one's enemy by making the forgiving old king look foolish and deserving of death.
The Helen scene teaches that love is a source of evil, destruction, and corruption, directly reversing the Odyssey's claim that love heals the shattered person and leads one home.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated interpretation of the Aeneid/Odyssey contrast.

claims.claim: Roman priority orders family as patriarch first, inheriting son second, and wife as follower; Jiang contrasts this with the relative equality of Penelope and Odysseus.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated model of Roman family hierarchy.

claims.claim: Aeneas' role is service to destiny and hierarchy rather than mutual equality in a relationship.

signature_moments.moment: Roman poetry becomes almost pornographic in violence, then retrains the reader to sneer at forgiveness.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the aesthetic method by which Jiang thinks the Aeneid corrupts Homer.

signature_moments.moment: The Odyssey says love takes the shattered person home; the Aeneid says love destroys civilization and leads to hell.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the sharpest moral inversion of the episode.

signature_moments.moment: Aeneas carries father and son while the wife follows, making the family itself a political order.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The most important person in the family is the patriarch
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It converts an escape scene into Jiang's model of Roman hierarchy.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's formulation of the Aeneid's anti-Odyssey lesson: love corrupts, destroys civilization, and leads away from salvation.
The hierarchy of father, son, and wife/follower that Jiang extracts from Aeneas' escape from Troy.
Jiang's term for Virgilian violence that invites fascination and moral retraining rather than Homeric reconciliation.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang's phrase "the Iliad" in this stretch appears to refer to the Aeneid's negation of the Iliad; ASR and lecture shorthand blur the book titles.
